title: Buying and storing hay for winter
subtitle: How to assess hay quality, calculate how much to buy, and store it to prevent spoilage.
excerpt: Buying hay in summer costs less than buying it in November when everyone is short. Getting the storage right determines whether that hay is still good in February or whether a third of it has composted itself in the corner of a barn.
sourceNotes: AHDB, forage quality and storage guidance. SAC Consulting, hay and silage management notes. ADAS, forage conservation technical notes.

content.text: Hay quality is set at the time it is cut and baled. Once you have bought it, you can only maintain what is there; not improve it. Buying early from a good crop at the right stage gives you more options than buying in desperation in autumn from whoever still has stock.
What to look for when buying

Good hay should smell clean and grassy, not musty, mouldy, or fermented. It should be a green to golden colour; hay that is brown throughout has been rained on before or after baling. Break open a bale and feel the core: if it is noticeably warmer than the outside, the hay is still heating and its
content is too low. Visible mould or black patches on bale cores mean the hay has already degraded. For a large purchase, a
gives a precise picture of nutritional value and dry matter content.

A simple per-animal guide: a dry ewe needs approximately 1 to 1.5 kg of hay per day; a horse 8 to 10 kg per day; a beef cow 8 to 12 kg per day; a goat 1 to 2 kg per day. Estimate the number of days of supplementary feeding needed (typically November to April in the UK), multiply by the daily requirement, and add 20 per cent for waste and safety margin. A small round bale weighs 200 to 350 kg; a large round bale 300 to 500 kg; square bales 20 to 25 kg each.

Store hay off the ground on pallets or on a straw base, to prevent ground moisture wicking upward. Under cover is best; if storing outside, use plastic sheeting that extends well down the sides and is held in place against wind. The sides of an outdoor stack are the most vulnerable to water ingress; lean square bales on edge around the perimeter so any surface wetting hits the bale face rather than the core.

Feed hay from a hay rack rather than on the ground to reduce waste and contamination from feet and droppings. Position racks so animals cannot stand in them or drop hay and eat it from the floor beneath, which increases worm egg ingestion in sheep and goats.
